(b) Certification Plan/Compliance Plan; and 
(c) Detailed project description. 
5.2 Review Eligibility of Application 
(1) The appli cation will be re viewed ag ainst Cha pter 513 of the AWM to verify that the applicant  
meets th e el igibility req uirements an d that t he inte nded hold er ha s the  abi lity to meet the 
continuing airworthiness requirements. 
(2) Considerations for type design changes intended for military applications or for aircraft operating 
outside the jurisdiction of a civil aviation authority are as follows:  
(a)  TCCA does not supp ort appli cations for the ap proval of m odifications exclusively 
intended for Canadian DND operated aircraft, as well as other military-only use aircraft; 
(b) On aircraft operated outside the jurisdiction of a civil aviation authority, the issuance of an 
STC may only be pursued under the following conditions: 
(i) the aircraft type h as a Canadian Type Certificate or an equivalent foreign Type 
Certificate acceptable to TCCA for the issuance of a Certificate of Airworthiness; 
(ii) the modificati on doe s not  involve m ilitary equipm ent that does not have civil  
certification and that affects or interfaces with the aircraft systems; and 
(iii) there is a clear potential f or direct ci vil application of the modification o n other 
aircraft of the same type. 
(c) On aircraft operated as a civil-registered aircraft, the is suance of an LSTC or STC may 
be pursued for “special mission” modifications only if there exists a clear civil application. 
(3) An example of an installat ion meeting t he above criteria would b e the installation of a Tech nical 
Standard Order Flig ht Manag ement S ystem on a Canadair Ch allenger CL-600 op erated by a  
foreign military organization. 
(4)  Applicants will be notified if the above criteria are not met. 
(5) Special considerations for manned free balloons—Su rface or air transport of hot air ballo on fuel 
cells (gas cylinders) is an inherent characteristic of hot air balloon operation.  It is re commended 
that hot air b alloon operators be aware of the regula tory requirements applicable to the surf ace 
and ai r tra nsport of the f uel ga s cylinders, in cluding the requirements of t he T DG A ct and  
Regulations.  Applicant s are remi nded that t he CARs addre ss only the approv al and use of the  
fuel cylin ders while on aircraft for p urposes pursuant to the  Aero nautics Act.  Th e TDG 
requirements apply and m ust be followed when the fuel gas cylinders are in transport by ro ad or 
any mode within the scop e of the TDG Act.  The TDG Regulations require that contai ners for 
compressed gas me et and be ce rtified to pr escribed sta ndards (CAN/ CSA B339 with TC 
stamping).  For this reason, concurrent certification under the CARs and under TDG is desirable 
for the fuel cylinder.  It is the gas cylinder manufacturer’s responsibility to apply for certification of  
the cylinders to the Director, Regulatory Affairs, TDG, and it is up to each cylinder user to request 
a properly certified cylinder from his respective manufacturer or supplier.   
5.3 NAP A 
(1) The NAPA system is used to generate a NAPA project number.  A hard copy file s hould also be 
created using the NAPA project file number.  The TCCA projec t engineer is to use the NAPA 
database to  log all p roject a pplications, and re cord p roject data, which sh all in clude a s 
appropriate, the app roval number, the applicant and holder n ames, the proje ct title/description, 
and the type, model, and serial numbers of the affected aeronautical products. 
(2) NAPA is accessible externally by delegates using the NAPA-NDWL.  The NAPA-NDWL assists in 
the processing of aeronautical product approvals by allowing delegates the ability to directly enter 
data into the NAPA database.  Access to the N APA-NDWL is limited to projects managed by the 
individual delegate.  Access rights to the system through the NAPA-NDWL vary by the delegate.

5.4 Fees 
(1) Pursuant to section 1 04.03 and of the CARs, t he issue of an STC, an LSTC or an RDC requires 
the holder to pay a fixed fee. 
(2) Section 104. 04 of the CA Rs, CA D No.  3 and SI G EN-004 provi de spe cifics on fees and  co st 
recovery as they pertain to aircraft certification.  
5.5 Project Assessment 
(1) The project assessment process is to b e carried out by consulting the flowcha rt in Appendix  A, 
and using the factors outlined in Appendix B. 
(2) The RMAC shall ensure that all proje cts are a ssessed by the a ppropriate project engineer(s), in 
order to determine the level of technical expertise and specialist involvement outside their region.  
Expertise may be sought either from HQ or another region. 
(3) Consultation with other regional offices and HQ during the project assessment period is beneficial 
and may be needed to en sure that co mpliance to the appli cable airworthiness requirements is 
found consistently in a  n ational context.  In a ddition, this early communi cation may avoid  any 
problematic issues by providing early resolution and documentation to the problem. 
5.6 Hea dquarters Notification 
(1) The RMAC should notify the Chief, Pro ject Management Division where a project meets any of 
the criteria in Appendix B and the knowledge and expertise does not exist in t he region.  S ome 
communication may be necessary to ensure national consistency in the application of standards.  
The informati on in the form in  Appendix C needs to  be communicated to facilitate the proper 
coordination of projects.  Completing the Appendix C Form, including the regional proposal for the 
management of the proj ect, provid es backg round information  to the Project Man agement 
Division. 
(2) The Project Management Division will review the regional proposal and  shall notify the reg ion 
and initiate d iscussions with the app ropriate RMAC to determi ne the app ropriate delineation of 
project management responsibility, including the assignment of specialist resources. 
5.7 Requests for Assistance 
(1) Where it is determined that the proj ect will be m anaged by the regio nal office, with a ssistance 
from othe r re gions or HQ, a formal request fo r assi stance should be ma de b y completin g the 
Appendix C Form.  The completed F orm shall be fo rwarded to the Proje ct Management Division 
and/or RMAC for co ordination and a ction with th e respon sible HQ speci alists an d/or RMAC 
involved. 
(2) Where a ssistance is required from HQ, Proje ct Manag ement sho uld be provided wit h the  
proposed certification basis and means of compliance.   
(3) Where the development of an SCA is required for certain elements of the project, the Regulatory 
Standards Division should be consulted. 
(4) Findings of compliance made by HQ or regional specialists for specific elements of the project are 
to be provided to the regional office responsible for the management of the project.  
5.8 Project Transfer 
The transfer of a project to another region or HQ requires prior consultation, between the parties 
involved, at the management level.  Where it is determined that the project will be transferred, the 
complete documentation pa ckage is to b e fo rwarded to  th e a ssigned region or P roject 
Management Division as appropriate.  The region that originally received the a pplication should 
then advise the applicant, in writing, where the project has been transferred, including the name 
of a contact person.

5.9 Headquarters Flight Test Involvement 
The Flight T est Divisi on will prov ide the regions with support asso ciated wit h flight evaluation,  
required inputs, human f actors elements, and review of the Aircra ft Flight Man ual Supplements 
(AFMS).  The relevant guidance material is contained in A C 513-003, AC 51 3-004 and SI 513-
008. 
5.10 TCCA Approval and Acceptance of Documents 
(1) Within this SI, the term “approv e” is limited to ce rtificates such as the ST C, the LSTC, a nd the 
RDC, as well as the Airworthiness Limitation (AWL) section of an ICA, the AFMS , and installation 
repair instructions, such as an Engineering Order. 
(2) An acceptance is an acknowledgement by TCCA that an item complies or that a plan should lead 
to compli ance.  For exam ple, engin eering rep orts, MSI 53 che cklists and te st plans woul d be  
accepted. 
(3) For project management purposes stamps and signatures as appropriate shall be used to record 
approvals.  Project documentation and decision registers are to be kept to record   acceptances. 
6.0 C HANGED PRODUCT RULE 
Application o f the Chang ed Prod uct Rule (CPR ) is part of the pro cess in  determinin g the  
certification basis for a change to t he type de sign re sulting from an aircraft ce rtification 
modification approval acti vity.  PL 500-002 a nd AC 500-016 provide more de tailed information 
regarding the use of CPR. 
6.1 Applicant CPR Roles and Responsibilities 
(1) Applicants are responsible for identifying and evaluating the proposed change to the type design.  
Applicants who have been granted the privilege of determining whether a change is significant, or 
not, nee d to include th e CPR decision re cord in the certification data.  Note, this privilege i s 
typically granted to delegates. Those applicants who do not h ave this privilege should prepare a 
draft proposal of the CPR decision assessment. 
(2) The applicant needs to ide ntify if the change i s significant or not significant at the pro duct level.  
The determination of whether a cha nge is significant or not significant includes a review of any 
previous relevant design change. 
(3) The ap plicant must apply  the latest regulatio ns fo r sig nificant cha nges, unl ess a propo sal is 
presented to apply earlie r regulatio ns.  The appli cant is to prop ose a certification ba sis a t the 
appropriate amendment levels. 
(4) The CP R decisi on re cord form is avai lable through NAPA and shou ld be completed through 
NAPA. 
6.2 Aircraft Certification Personnel CPR Roles and Responsibilities 
Aircraft Certification personnel are responsible to: 
(a) Guide the applicant on application of the rule.  The Regulatory Standards Division of the 
Aircraft Certification Branch will act as the HQ contact point and will be responsibl e for 
leading and maintaining the National Standardization Team, and provide CPR specialist 
support; 
(b) Ensure that t he NAPA sy stem is updat ed to reflect the CP R decision and that the CPR 
decision record form available through NAPA has been used to record the decision; 
(c) Use the i ssue pa per p rocess to record i ssues and resolution s for chan ges to th e 
certification basis.  Refer to SI 500-019 for guidance on issue papers; 
(d) Accept the certification basis; and

(e) Document the certification basis in the amended STC, or LSTC.  This would be repeated 
for ame nded STCs an d L STCs.  S ee Appendix D and E fo r gu idance o n fo rmat an d 
content.  The document record is retained in the NAPA project file. 
6.3 Delegate CPR Documentation Requirements 
A delegate with the app ropriate scop e of authorit y for a particula r proj ect, who ha s met the 
conditions to make a det ermination of significa nt or not significant, may in accordance with 
delegate’s approved manual make the determination and submit that as part of their a pplication 
process.  When applying for an approval, the applicant will submit the TCCA form 26-0469 along 
with the CPR decision record form available through NAPA.  
6.4 Recording the Certification Basis 
 Any modification to a type desi gn that chan ges a prod uct’s certification  basi s mu st be 
documented on the STC or LSTC.  The ce rtification basi s for modification s to type design  
approvals recorded o n a  certific ate requires that  the applica nt docum ent the airwo rthiness 
standards, as well as the appropriate amendment levels. 
7.0 MAINTENANCE AND MANUFACTURING INVOLVEMENT 
This section describes the main areas where the aircraft certification activities interact with aircraft 
maintenance and manufacturing activities during certification projects. 
7.1 Instructions for Continued Airworthiness 
(1) As required by the certification basis for the product with the type design change, there may be a 
need to dev elop and review an ICA .  Aircraft maintena nce and manuf acturing pe rsonnel 
involvement should be pursued early on for the review and acceptance of ICAs. 
(2) ICA documents are not approved, however, the AWLs must be approved by the RMAC.  Relevant 
guidance material for the review of ICAs is found in MSI 53. 
7.2 Con formity Inspections 
(1) The a pplicant is respon sible in accordance with Chapter 513 of the A WM, for en suring full 
conformity of  all m aterials, pa rts, processes, construction a nd assembly to  the type  de sign 
drawings and specifications, for all approval projects. 
(2) TCCA i s re sponsible un der the STC, LSTC an d RDC a pproval process to verify, with a high 
degree of confidence, t he ap plicant’s compli ance with th e applicable ai rworthiness and 
environmental requirements and conformity of t he materials, parts, processes, construction and 
assembly to the type design configuration.   F ollowing receipt of documentary evidence that the 
applicant’s d esignated Ai rcraft Mainte nance En gineer (AME), Aircraft Main tenance Officer, 
(AMO), or suitable Quality Assurance Representative has carried out a conformity inspection, the 
TCCA Civil Aviation Safety Inspector (CASI) may carry out a conformity inspe ction.   Confidence 
in verification is gained by conducting TCCA conformity inspections on a random basis. 
(3) During approval program s, situations may aris e where conform ity inspections will be required.  
Appendix M describes situations that would warrant a conformity inspection.  A TCCA conformity 
inspection is not a requi rement for all certification a ctivities.  A TCCA conformity inspe ction is 
above and beyond the company conformity inspection, which is always required. 
(4) Where required, co nformity inspe ctions are to  b e reque sted, by  the Proje ct Enginee r to the 
Regional Manager, Aircraft Maintenance and Manufacturing for coordination with the designated 
inspector.  The inspe ctor shoul d be p rovided with the name of  a conta ct p erson a nd p hone 
number for the applicant, details of th e defined design configuration and details of the item s for 
which conformity is required.   The results of the conformity in spections are to be provide d, in 
writing, to the project engineer for review, with a copy placed on the project file.

7.3 Issuance of Flight Permit 
(1) A major modification is installed on an aircraft with a maintenance procedure.  It may not be flown 
again until a maintenance release has been issued, except where a flight permit is in effect for 
that purpose.  Since an AME may not sign a mai ntenance release for an unapproved installation, 
a flight permi t will be required.  Under these circum stances, the Project Engineer is to forward  
appropriate conditions a nd/or limitation s, whi ch a re to be come an  integral pa rt of the requi red 
flight authority.  The conditions and/or limitations are normally requested by the CASI. 
(2) The CASI will issue a flight permit after a satisfactory review of the pertinent documentation.  The 
documentation includes conditions and/or limitations prescribed by the Project Engineer.  
8.0 INVOLVEMENT OF OTHER CIVIL AVIATION FUNCTIONAL REPRESENTATIVES     
For projects involving the approval of equipment that is required to meet operational requirements 
prescribed in  Part VI and Part VII of the CA Rs, it may be necessa ry to con sult with  the  
appropriate functional personnel within the regions or HQ to e nsure th at such install ations are 
suitable to meet the intended operational requirements. 
9.0 ACCEPTANCE OF CERTIFICATION PLAN 
(1) The TCCA project engineer w ill accept a certification plan that  meets the objectives of AC 500-
015.  The RMAC should be consulted when the certification plan involves a large commitment 
from the regional TCCA office.  Early acceptance of a certification plan is needed to ensure an: 
(a) Agreed upon LOI is established; 
(b) Agreed project schedule is established; and 
(c) Agreement on the means and methods to show compliance. 
(2) The certificat ion plan should in clude if po ssible, feedba ck from Aircraft Maintena nce and  
Manufacturing personnel for ICA review and conformity inspections, or other operational groups 
for scheduling purposes. 
(3) Smaller p rojects m ay hav e a scal ed d own ve rsion of a ce rtification plan if a n agreem ent is 
reached in advance by the TCCA project engineer. 
10.0 DEMONSTRATION AND RECORDING OF COMPLIANCE 
This section deals with th e variou s m eans used by the appli cant and/or TCCA that cont ribute 
towards the demonstration of compliance and the TCCA expectations associated with processing 
and recording compliance. 
10.1 Testing 
(1) Where testing is being conducted that contributes to the demonstration of compliance, this testing 
is to be considered "certification testing". TCCA acceptance of a test plan is required to ensure 
the acceptance of data generated from certification testing. Applicants should be cautioned that if 
the test plan i s not accepted or if TCCA’s witnessing requirements are not satisfied before a test 
is conducted, TCCA may not accept the test results.   
(2) A test pla n identifies the test apparatus, test vehicle and configuration, conformity requirements, 
witnessing requirements, test details including conditions and pass/fail criteria, data requirements, 
and hazard level with risk mitigation actions.  Test plans should be accepted as early as possible 
and prior to the test.   
(3) Test plan s are written by the applicant and accepted by th e rele vant TCCA Project Engi neer.  
Time for TCCA review of test plans must be provided.

(4) Specialists (HQ or regional) witness tests in order to assess the design and to make a finding of 
compliance.  Norm ally, when T CCA chooses to witness a test,  the relevant  delegate will b e 
present as well.   
(5) TCCA spe cialists do not p articipate in  t he te sts, ex cept in ve ry u nique ci rcumstances such as  
flight-testing.  This ensu res that the indivi dual re mains imp artial and ca n con centrate on the 
overall activity rather than  being  taske d with performing a specific fun ction while th e te st is 
ongoing. 
10.2 Engineering Inspections 
(1) An engineering inspection is a specific task carried out to review the finished product against the 
design requi rements.  An engineering i nspection is  not a confo rmity inspe ction.  A co nformity 
inspection is required prior to an engineering inspection. 
(2) Some reasons for an engineering inspection are: 
(a) To give perspective to the drawings and determine their adequacy; 
(b) To provide familiarization with the aircraft including, but not limited to the layout, systems 
operations and structural load paths; 
(c) To show possible interactions and interference between systems or components; 
(d) To examin e HIRF or li ghtning related  feature s such as bonding o r ga ps i n metalli c 
enclosures; 
(e) To conduct zonal inspections for the purposes of compliance to standards fo r flammable 
fluids, fire zones or interior installations; and 
(f) To verify compliance with the requirements of an airworthiness standard. 
(3) TCCA specialists or delegates conducting an engineering inspection should document the results 
and n ote any  discrepan cies resulting from de sign or confo rmity issue s a s a ction item s for the 
applicant.  T he applicant should be debriefed on th e inspection and the action items recorded.  
The debrief should identify any a reas of non-compliance and follow-up should be undertaken to 
confirm that deficiencies have been corrected. 
10.3 Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement Review  
An approved AFMS may be a requi rement to ensu re compliance to the certifica tion basis of the 
aircraft.  The regional project engineer appr oves the AFMS.  AC 513-004 and AC 500-014 
provide further guidance on FMS documents.   
10.4  Instructions for Continued Airworthiness Review 
Aircraft certif ication p ersonnel shoul d requ est that  aircraft mai ntenance a nd manufa cturing 
personnel to review the ICA docum ents, prior to  acceptance. Airworthiness Limitations (AWLs) 
sections are approved by TCCA, aircraft certification personnel on behalf of the Mini ster.  The 
Minister’s delegate approving the design change will make the final determination as to whether 
to make the approval conditional upon compliance with these items. Relevant guidance material 
can be found in MSI 53. Aircraft mainte nance and manufacturing personnel should be forwarded 
the approp riate draft do cument, and  other rel ated document s for review early on in the 
certification program so that it may be reviewed in parallel with  aircraft certification activities.   
10.5 Master Minimum Equipment List  
An app roved Maste r Mini mum Equi pment List (MMEL) o r MEL is not a req uirement for the 
issuance of an approval. However, in the event one is requested, refer to the Flight Test Division 
and TP 9155 for guidance. It is a beneficial tool for in-service issues.

10.6 Substa ntiation Review 
As established in the  LOI f or TCCA findings of compliance, the p roject engineer will ensure that 
the app ropriate docu ments, dra wings and sub stantiation are TCCA revie wed or inde pendently 
reviewed.  These d ocuments may in clude structural a nalysis and electrical analy sis a mong 
others. 
10.7 Technical Issue Papers 
(1) An issue pa per (IP) documents a techni cal i ssue on a dom estic p roject, whi ch indi cates the  
possible existence of non -compliance to one o r more related elements of the certification basis 
for that project. 
(2) An IP may be used when:  
(a) TCCA a nd th e appli cant cannot re ach agre ement on a subje ct.  The proj ect engineer 
should attempt to resolve the issue prior to it becoming an IP; and 
(b) The de sign or me ans of  compli ance is not tradit ional, or i s of a novel o r unu sual 
approach.  In this context, the IP serves as a recording medium to give special visibility to 
that aspect of the design even though there is no disagreement between the delegate or 
TCCA. 
(3) The project engineer is responsible for: 
(a) Raising or initiating the issue paper; 
(b) Sign-off by the appropriate technical specialists; 
(c) Agreement on action items; 
(d) Ensuring that the IP is closed and stored as a compliance record 
(4) The appropriate guidance for the creation of issue papers can be found in SI 500-019. 
10.8 SCA Development 
Refer to SI 500-004 for guidance on the creation of SCAs.  The SCA shall be recorded as part of 
the basis of certification.  The development of th e SCA is do cumented in a G-2 IP.  Refe r to SI  
500-019 for the format of the G-2 IP document. 
10.9 Exemptions  
(1) An applicant may petition the Minister for an Exemption from a particular portion of a standard or 
regulation.  The applicant is responsible to pay all fees for Exemption, where so identified. 
(2) An Exemption may be issued where it is the opinion of the Minister to be in the public interest and 
not likely to affect publi c safety.  CA D No. 1 and CAD No. 3 0 provid e the  appli cable p olicy 
regarding ex emptions.  E xemptions are do cumented in a G-5 IP. Refer to  SI 500-0 19 f or the  
format of the G-5 IP document. 
(3) The Director, Aircraft Certification can approve exemptions to design standards. 
10.10 Findings of Equivalent Safety  
A Finding of Equivalent Safety may be  needed where the usually accepted or industry standard 
means of compliance for a particular airworthiness requirement is not t he one proposed by the 
applicant.  An FES is documented in a  G-3 IP. Re fer SI 500-01 9 for the format of the G-3 IP 
document.

10.11 Fuel Tank Systems Fault Tolerance Evaluation Requirements 
10.11.1 Background 
(1) Following the  1996 TWA accident, whi ch was cau sed by an explosion in th e ce ntre wing fuel  
tank, the  FA A pro mulgated rulema king to e stablish seve ral ne w tran sport aeroplane f uel tank 
safety requirements.  In the United States (US), FAA Special Federal Aviation Regulation No. 88 
(Part 21 Amd t. 21-78) ap plies to holde rs of, and applicants for, Type Ce rtificates and STCs that 
may affect th e ae roplane fuel tan k system, for turbi ne-powered transport cate gory a eroplanes, 
provided the type certificat e was issued after January 1, 1958, and the aeropl ane has either a 
maximum typ e certificate d pa ssenger capacity of  30  or more, or a maximu m type certificated 
payload capacity of 3400 kg (7,500 lbs) or more. 
(2) Each type certificate holder, or supplemental type certificate holder of a modification affecting the 
aeroplane fuel tank system, was to accomplish the following: 
(a) Conduct a sa fety review of  the aeroplan e fuel tank system to det ermine that the de sign 
meets the requirements o f Federal Aviation Regulation (FA R) P art 25 (Amdt. 25-10 2) 
subsections 25.901 and 25.981 (a) and (b).  If the  current design does not m eet these 
requirements, develop all design changes to the fuel tanks system that are necessary to 
meet these requirements; and 
(b) Develop all maintena nce and insp ection instructio ns ne cessary to maintain the desi gn 
features required to preclude the exi stence or development of an ignition source within 
the fuel tanks system of the aeroplane. 
(3) These reviews were a “re-evaluation” of previously approved fuel tank systems using the F AR 
Part 25 Amdt . 25-102 fuel tank system ignition p revention airworthiness standards.  The se fuel 
tank ignition prevention airworthiness standards were introduced in Chapter 525 of th e AWM, , 
effective 2002/08/19,  published at Change 525-11 as discussed in the Preamble to Chapter 525 
of the AWM. 
10.11.2 Canadian Requirements 
(1) At the time of issuan ce of this SI, ther e continues to b e no  Tran sport Canada regulatory 
requirement for existin g Canadian holders of Type Certificates and STCs to  conduct a fuel  tank 
system fa ult toleran ce re-evaluation against t he revised AWM fuel tan k safety stan dards.  
Equally, there is n o retroactive applicability a pplied by  re gulation specifically to subsection 
525.981(a) and (b) or Appendix H525.4 of the AWM.  Regulatory amendments are proceeding via 
the CARAC process.  However, it mu st be recognized that the applicable Canadian holders of 
applicable certificates have nonetheless completed design reviews in accordance with the SFAR 
No.88 requirements on a voluntary basis.  As a result, numerous design changes and instructions 
for continued airworthiness, including Limitations, have been developed and promulgated most 
importantly by Airworthiness Directive. Much knowledge of the specific vulnerabilities of fuel tank 
system designs with respect to the developme nt of ignition sources was g ained during the world 
fleet design review activity, to the extent that TCCA, in concert with other world civil airworthiness 
authorities and agencies, have now recognized that failure to adhere to the new best practices 
identified by the amended fuel tank safety requirements (AWM Change 525-11) would constitute 
an unacceptable and unsafe condition, to the extent that issu ance of a d esign approval or other 
certificate or approval should not be permitted  where complia nce with the se requi rements is 
absent. 
(2) There are several requi rements within the CA Rs that safegu ard ag ainst the issuan ce of 
certificates and approvals that would not meet the requireme nts of 525.901, 525.981(a) and (b) 
and Appendix H525.4 (Change 525-11) of the AWM in this instance, some examples being: 
(a) paragraph 511.11(1)(b) of the CARs, “no feature or characteristic makes the aeronautical 
product unsafe”;

(b) paragraph 5 13.07(3)(b) o f the CARs, “t he assumptions used in obtaining the type 
certificate for the aerona utical product do not remai n valid.”  Thi s is p erhaps the most 
applicable ex isting regulation relating t o STC, LST C an d RDC issuance, whereas th e 
earlier assumptions related to fuel tank system ignition source vulnerabilities have now 
been recognized a s n ot valid, with new b est p ractices a nd standards n ow bei ng 
recognized. 
(c) paragraph 513.07(4)(b) of the CARs, by Changed Product Rule (CPR) principles, earlier 
standards than those introduced by Chapter 525 of the AWM (p ublished at Change 525-
11) for new changes to a type design affecting fuel tank system safety could only be used 
if to do so “…wo uld not contribute materially to the level of safety…”.  The preclu sion of 
the existence and development of ignition sources within the fuel tank system is deemed 
to materially affect the level safety. 
(d) section 525.601 of the AWM, an aeroplane shall not ”have design features or details that 
experience h as shown to  be h azardous or unreliable”.  T he fu el tank sy stem safety 
activity has revealed certain previous designs to be hazardous or unreliable, to the extent 
that a catast rophic event could o ccur.  Care  should be taken to a dopt the ne w fuel tank 
safety sta ndards p ublished in  Ch apter 5 25 of th e AWM  (Ch ange 525 -11) an d b est 
practices discussed in the associated guidance material. 
(e) subsection 507.02(c) of t he  CA R Sta ndard, and  a ircraft mu st b e one “that is safe for 
flight”, in the case of issuance a certificate of airworthiness.  To the extent that corrective 
action h as been ta ken on the wo rld fleet by  means of Airwo rthiness Directives, 
modifications  to affected installations that do not comply with AWM 525.901, 525.981(a) 
and (b) a nd Appendix H525.4 of th e AWM (as p ublished at Chang e 5 25-11) m ay be  
considered unsafe. 
(f) section 507.11 of the CAR Standard , “aircraft continues to meet the conditions subject to 
which the flight authority was issued” (e.g. subsection 507.02(c) of the CARs), in order for 
a certificate of airworthiness to remain in force.  Therefore, the incorporation of an unsafe 
feature would invalidate the certificate of airworthiness. 
(3) All ongoing and new certification and a pproval activities for tran sport category aeroplanes shall 
comply with subsections 525.981 (a) and (b ), section 525.901 and  Appendix H, H525.4 of the 
AWM.  A F uel Ta nk Sa fety Impact Assessment Checklist i s shown in Ap pendix L  of this 
document.  Design changes should be assessed using this che cklist to determine if th ey could 
ignite flammable vapours in, or adjacent to, the fuel tank system. 
(4) The b asis of ce rtification for ea ch ST C, LST C an d RD C is to  be  es tablished a nd r ecorded to  
include section 525.901, 525.981(a) and (b) and Appendix H525.4 at Change 525-11 (equivalent 
to FAR Part 25 at Amendment 25-102).

findings of compliance and to issue the certificate.

certification plan.  AE-10 0 forms are a  stateme nt of com pliance to identify do cuments used to 
substantiate and identify the design. This Form does not approve the referenced documents.

11.2 Transport Canada Civil Aviation Staff 
The project engineer will make a findi ng of comp liance for the se ctions where they have a LOI  
and a fin ding may be required.  Thi s woul d be as sp ecified i n the certification pla n or LOI.  
Initialling the CP is a good checklist procedure to ensure that all areas have been looked at, but a 
TCCA signature on the fi nal approv al certificate will signify that TCCA i s satisfied that ov erall 
compliance has been demonstrated.   
11.3 Consolidate Compliance Records 
The p roject engineer will  co nsolidate the comp liance re cords, do cumentation, e-mails and 
decision records.  The compliance program will be reviewed to ensure that all the sections have 
been signed off.  At that point the documents to be listed on an STC will be approved as required 
in preparation for STC issuance.  For all LSTC, this may be done by the delegate. 
12.0 PREPARATION OF STC, LSTC AND RDC APPROVAL DOCUMENTS 
12.1 Aircra ft Certification Personnel 
Aircraft Certification personnel will use the NAPA system to generate the appropriate approval 
certificates.  These certificates consist of a number of fields consisting of the phrases identified in 
Appendix D.  An example of an STC can be found in Appendix E. 
12.2 Deleg ates 
Delegates who have access to t he NAPA-NDWL will use the system to issue their certificates.  
These certificates will also use App endix D a s appropriate to ensure national consistency in the 
creation of certificates.  Appen dix E may be u sed as general guidance to h elp delegates format 
their delegated LSTC and RDC projects. 
13.0 ISSUANCE OF APPROVAL DOCUMENTS 
13.1 Issuanc e by Transport Canada 
(1) Following a satisfactory review of su pporting dat a again st de fined airwo rthiness standards, 
including the  resolution o f any non-compliant items, the re sponsible p roject engi neer shall  
approve the design chan ge or re pair b y issuin g an LSTC, RDC or STC, a s appli cable.  The 
project engineer may use the checklist in Appendix G as a last quality check. 
(2) An LSTC shall only i nclude aircraft owned or operated by th e same individual or organization.  
This requirement applies irrespective of the person holding the certificate.  This applies equally to 
RDCs, except when issuing a “process RDC”. 
13.2 Issuance by a Delegate 
(1) Where a satisfactory review has been completed, a delegate shall approve and issue an LSTC or 
an RDC.  T he restrictions in th e previous section of this document apply equally to del egates.  
The may use the checklist in Appendix G as a last quality check. 
(2) For projects which are fully delegated, except fo r the approval of the flight ma nual supplement 
and/or AWL, the associ ated certificate may still be signed by the delegate, and includ e the “/D” 
suffix in the number.   
(3) To en sure consi stency b etween ce rtificates issue d by delegate s and th ose issued by T CCA, 
certificate forms used by delegates shall be those provided by the appropriate regional office, or 
those produced in accordance with electronic templates approved by TCCA aircraft certification.  
13.3 Issuance of Canadian Approval to a Foreign Certificate Holder by TCCA 
(1) Canadian approvals should not be issued directly to foreign applicants.  T his is in contradiction 
with the bilat eral a greements and does not ad equately place t he re sponsibility for conti nuing 
airworthiness with the airworthiness authorit y that has ov ersight responsibility for that 
organization that holds the certificate, i.e. the State of Design obligations as per ICAO principles.

agreement b etween the  concerned a uthorities o n co operation with respect to continuing 
airworthiness responsibiliti es, since the  certific ate holder is under the ju risdiction of the other 
authority.  Under 3.0.2.0(3) of  the TCCA/FAA BASA IP, the only scenario that the FAA will i ssue 
an STC to a Canadian organization without a corresponding TCCA STC upon which to base it is: 
“In special cases at the  req uest of TCCA. The FAA may a ccept 
applications from Cana dian companies who do not h old an eq uivalent 
Canadian S TC be cause the spe cific model of t he produ ct to be  
modified has no Canadian TC. Thes e cases will only be considered 
from applicants who hol d other Cana dian approvals for similar d esign 
changes a nd are u nder TCCA oversight. The ap plication pa ckage 
should be forwarded to the New York Aircraft Certification Office with a 
letter from TCCA confirming that the applicant meets these conditions.”  
13.4 Issuance of a Canadian Approval to a Foreign Certificate Holder by a TCCA Delegate 
(1) A TCCA delegate cannot issue an approval directly to a foreign certificate holder.  The approval 
shall only be issued to a Canadian individual or organization.  Canada is considered the State of 
Design for th e modification.  T CCA would then make application to the fo reign authority (as per 
relevant agreement) to issue a corresponding foreign approval document.  Canada would remain 
the State of Design at this point.  The certificate holder would then apply to TCCA to transfer both 
foreign and domestic approvals to the new holder.  Refer to SI 500-018 for further details.   
(2) The inverse is also possible for the case of a FAA designee being involved and the final approval 
holder being a Canadian individual or organization. 
13.5 Issuance of Incremental Modification Approvals 
(1) Multiple LST Cs m ay be  issu ed to support the  incremental  installation  and app roval of 
modifications.  The final LSTC that is issued will unite the entire modification package together by 
identifying al l the appli cable LST Cs and re -examining all compliance aspects of th e full 
installation for com pleteness.  Fo r exa mple, an LS TC may be i ssued t hat o nly approves the 
structural a spects of  an  avionics i nstallation without the avionics o r sy stems as pects being  
addressed.  At a later date, a se cond LSTC may be issued to a pprove the avionics or systems 
aspects with the second LSTC to unite the entire system.  
(2) Alternatively, a single L STC o r ST C can be  reissue d to cover p rogressively mo re of an  
installation.  For example, Issue 1 may cover the structural installation of an avionics system only 
without approved operation.  Issue 2 may cover the addition of the electrical and systems aspects 
and permit Visual Flight Rules operations only.  Issue 3 may then convey the full operational use 
including Instrument Flight Rules operation.  This process is used to permit the prototype or test 
aircraft to return to service for interim intervals during a lengthy LSTC/STC certification program. 
13.6 Certificate Transmittal Letters 
A transmittal letter using the format shown in Appendix H shall accompany all certificates issued.

(a) Copies of signed approval documents; 
(b) Compli ance Program; 
(c) Certification Plans; 
(d) Copies of documentation referenced on approval documents including; 
(i) Aircraft Flight Manual Supplements;

14.0 OBLIGATIONS OF THE CERTIFICATE HOLDER 
This section is incl uded to aid the proje ct engineer in the oversi ght of the holder oblig ations as 
required. 
14.1 Service Difficulty Reporting 
The certificate holder shall establish a procedure to record all known reportable service difficulties  
for products that have left their quality control sy stem.  The certificate holder shall undertake the 
obligations outlined in Chapter 591 of the AWM, and report any failures, malfunctions or defects 
to TCCA.  AC 591-001 and ACSI 44 provide further information. 
14.2 Mandatory Changes Required to Rectify an Unsafe Condition 
If a chan ge to the de sign of the aerona utical product is re quired to rectify an u nsafe condition, 
which would result in the Minister issuing an Ai rworthiness Directive, the holder of the ap proval 
document shall: 
(a) Propose the appropriate corrective acti on and submit the re quired design change to the 
Minister for approval; and 
(b) On approval, make avail able to each known owner and air operator of th e aeronautical 
product, the information that is necessary to allow them to make the change. 
14.3 Manuals 
The certificate hol der sh all produ ce, maintain  a nd u pdate a ll manu als, supplements and  
subsequent amendments required by the certification basis and to support the operation of the 
aeronautical product in service. 
14.4 Instructions for Continued Airworthiness 
The certificate holder shall ensure that the quoted Supplemental ICA remains applicable. 
14.5 Design Changes, Drawing and Data Control 
The certificate holder shall have a system to control the design, drawing and document changes, 
to ensu re th at they are adeq uately controlle d, ap proved a nd inco rporated in the applicable 
drawings or change notices. The applicant shall demonstrate his system or procedures to control 
technical data and design changes to the Minister. 
14.6 Annotations of STCs by Stamping Procedures 
(1) The stamping of STCs by Canadian holders, as a means of controlling the use of their proprietary 
data or for any other reasons, is not acceptable to the Minister.

15.0 APPROVAL OF REVISIONS TO CERTIFICATES 
15.1 Cer tificate Transfers 
Refer to SI 500-018 for specific instructions on the transfer of certificates. 
15.2 Recording Changes on the Certificate 
(1) Revisions for the pu rpose of addin g m odels, addi tional serial numbers, references to new o r 
revised engineering data, or for in corporating other necessary changes or corrections, shall be 
annotated adjacent to the right hand margin to indicate the changes on the certificate. 
(2) Aircraft Certification personnel shall not reissue: 
(a) Certificates originally issued by a delegate; or 
(b) Certificates originally issued by another regional office, unless the associated project files 
have been transferred to the othe r region.  To  retain the origi ns of the approval, a note  
referencing the previous certificate should be added to the end of the STC/LST C or RDC 
certificate issued by the other region. 
15.3 Additional Requirements – LSTC and RDC Revisions 
An LSTC or RDC may b e revise d to add or d elete aircraft owned or o perated by the same  
individual or organization.  It may also be revised to reflect relate d design changes.  An aircraft  
may only be deleted from a “multiple serial number” LSTC or RDC where the aircraft in question 
is transferred to a new owner and TCCA has obtained proof that: 
(a) A sepa rate approval ha s been i ssued in the na me of the n ew ai rcraft o wner of the  
transferred aircraft; 
(b) The modification was removed from the aircraft prior to its transfer; or 
(c) A request for the approval  of the same  modification intended to be installed o n aircraft 
owned or operated by a separate individual has been submitted.

(1) TCCA shall forward a signed a nd completed application to the app ropriate Civil Aviation

(3) Given the v olume of p rojects with re quests for FAA and Europea n Aviation Safety Agen cy

Fuel Tank Safety Impact Assessment Form – Regional Approval Project Avionics /  
Electrical Design Changes, or 
Any Design Change that could ignite flammable vapours of the Fuel Tank System 
Addendum 
Intrinsically safe – Any instrument, equipment or wiring that is incapable of releasing sufficient electrical 
or thermal energy under normal operating or anticipated failure conditions (see paragraph   525.981(a)(3) 
of the AWM) to cause ignition within the fuel tank. 
Critical Design Configuration Control Limitation (CDCCL) – A design feature of an aeroplane, such as 
wire separation, explosion proof features of a fuel pump, maintenance intervals for transient suppression 
devices (TS D), minimu m bondin g jumpe r re sistance levels,  where any maintenan ce action s or 
subsequent changes to th e product made by ope rators or the m anufacturer MUST NOT DEGRADE th e 
level of safety of the original type design. 
Fuel System Airworthiness Limitation (FSL) –  An AWL a s derived from th e fuel ta nk system safety 
review, which contains the instru ctions for maint enance and inspec tion of  the fuel tank SYSTEM, 
including initial and repetitive inspection frequencies, required instruments, pass/fail criteria, etc., and any 
applicable critical design configuration control limitations (CDCCL) as required by Chapter 525 Appendix 
H 525.4 of the AWM. 
 
Section 525.981 Fuel Tank Ignition Prevention (amended 2002/08/19) 
(a) No ignition source may be present at each poi nt in the fuel tank o r fuel tank sy stem where 
catastrophic failure could occur due to ignition of fuel or vapours. This shall be demonstrated by: 
(1) Determi ning the high est tempe rature allo wing a safe margin belo w the lowe st 
expected auto-ignition temperature of the fuel in the fuel tanks. 
(2) Demonstrating that no  tem perature at each pla ce in side ea ch fuel ta nk where fu el 
ignition is possible will exceed the temperature determined under paragraph (a)(1) of this 
section. Thi s sh all be ve rified u nder all pr obable operating, fai lure, a nd m alfunction 
conditions of each component whose operation, failure, or malfunction could increase the 
temperature inside the tank. 
(3) Demonstrating that an ignition sou rce could not result from each single failure, fro m 
each sin gle failure in combination with eac h late nt failure conditi on not sh own to be  
extremely re mote, and from all comb inations of failure s n ot shown to be extremely 
improbable. The effect s of manufact uring va riability, aging, we ar, co rrosion, and likely 
damage shall be considered. 
(b) Ba sed o n the evalua tions requi red by this section, critical  desi gn configuration con trol 
limitations, in spections, o r other procedures shall be e stablished, as ne cessary, to p revent 
development of ignitio n sources withi n the fu el t ank sy stem and shall b e incl uded in  the 
Airworthiness Limitations section of th e Instru ctions for Continued Airwo rthiness requi red by  
525.1529. Visible means to identify critical featu res of the design shall be placed in areas of the 
aeroplane where  mainte nance a ctions, rep airs, or  a lterations ma y be apt to v iolate the critical 
design configuration limitations. 
(amended 2002/08/19; previous version) 
Additional Information:   
e.g., colour-coding of wire to identify separation limitation.
